title: Is a heat pump tumble dryer worth it?
description: Heat pump tumble dryers cost around 44p a load against £1.25 for a condenser. We work out the real payback for UK homes, plus the trade-offs.
blurb: Heat pump dryers cut drying costs by roughly two thirds. Here is when the higher price pays back.
lede: A tumble dryer is one of the hungriest appliances in the house, and the gap between the best and worst machines is enormous. A heat pump model dries the same load for roughly a third of the electricity a conventional condenser dryer uses, but it costs more to buy, takes longer per cycle, and needs a little more looking after. Whether that trade is worth making depends almost entirely on one number: how many loads you dry in a week. This guide puts real figures on the running costs, the payback time, and the practical differences, so you can decide with your own laundry habits in mind rather than a salesperson's.
short_answer: For most households that dry two or more loads a week, yes. A heat pump dryer uses around 1.7 kWh per full load against roughly 4.8 kWh for a condenser dryer, which works out at about 44p versus £1.25 at the current 26.11p per kWh cap rate. At three loads a week that saves around £126 a year, so a typical £150 to £250 purchase premium pays back in one to two years. If you only dry a handful of loads a month, the saving shrinks and a cheaper condenser model may still make sense.

sections.h2: How a heat pump dryer actually saves energy
sections.html:
A conventional dryer, whether vented or condenser, works like a giant hair dryer. An electric element of 2 to 2.5 kW heats air to around 70 to 75 degrees, blows it through the drum, and then throws most of that heat away, either out of a hose or into a condensing chamber. Every load means generating all that heat from scratch, which is why a single cycle can use 4 to 5 kWh.

A heat pump dryer keeps the heat instead of discarding it. It runs a small refrigeration circuit, the same technology as a fridge or an air source heat pump, in a closed loop. Warm, damp air leaves the drum, passes over a cold coil where the moisture condenses out into a tank, then passes over a hot coil where it is reheated and sent back into the drum. The same heat goes round and round, topped up by a compressor that typically draws only 600 to 900 watts.

The result is that a full 8 kg cotton load needs roughly 1.5 to 2 kWh rather than 4 to 5 kWh. The air is also cooler, usually 45 to 55 degrees, which is gentler on elastic, prints and wool-mix fabrics. The trade-off is time: moving heat is slower than brute-forcing it, so cycles typically run 30 to 60 minutes longer than on a condenser machine.

sections.h2: Running costs: heat pump vs condenser vs vented
sections.html:
Here is what a full-load cotton cycle costs on each dryer type, and what that adds up to over a year at three loads a week, which is close to the UK average for a family household. Figures assume a full 8 kg load; smaller or better-spun loads cost less on any machine.

Dryer typeEnergy per full loadCost per loadAnnual cost (3 loads a week)
Heat pump1.7 kWh44p£69
Vented4.2 kWh£1.10£171
Condenser4.8 kWh£1.25£196

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Energy per load is a typical figure for a full 8 kg cotton cycle; check the kWh per 100 cycles figure on any specific model's energy label.

The heat pump machine saves about 81p per full load against a condenser, or roughly £126 a year at three loads a week. Heavy users save more: at five loads a week the gap is around £210 a year. For a full breakdown of what the older machine types cost, including half loads and synthetics cycles, see our guide to tumble dryer running costs, and you can place the dryer alongside everything else in the house with our appliance running cost guide.

One small bonus: a modern heat pump dryer left plugged in draws only a watt or two when idle, so it barely registers in your standby power total. It is the cycles that cost money, not the socket.

sections.h2: The purchase premium and how fast it pays back
sections.html:
Entry-level heat pump dryers in the UK now start around £380 to £450, while basic condenser models start around £230 to £300. Call the premium £150 to £250 at the budget end; mid-range machines carry a similar gap. Prices have fallen a long way since heat pump dryers arrived, when the premium was £300 or more.

The payback sum is simple: divide the premium by your annual saving. Using the 81p per load saving from the table above, here is how a £200 premium plays out at different usage levels.

Loads per weekAnnual saving vs condenserPayback on a £200 premium
1£42Just under 5 years
2£84About 2.5 years
3£126About 1.6 years
5£210Under 1 year

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026, assuming 4.8 kWh per condenser load and 1.7 kWh per heat pump load.

Dryers commonly last 8 to 12 years, so even the one-load-a-week household comes out ahead eventually, just not quickly. From two loads a week upwards, the heat pump machine is clearly the cheaper choice over its life. If you are replacing a working condenser dryer purely to save energy, the maths is tighter: you are paying the full purchase price, not just the premium, so it rarely makes sense to scrap a healthy machine early. Buy the heat pump model when the old one dies or when you genuinely need a dryer for the first time.

sections.h2: What the energy label tells you, and what it hides
sections.html:
Heat pump dryers occupy the top bands of the dryer energy label; vented and condenser machines sit several classes below because the physics simply will not let a resistive heater compete. The most useful number on the label is not the letter but the energy consumption figure, quoted per 100 standard cycles on the newer label format, which lets you compare two machines directly: 150 kWh per 100 cycles means 1.5 kWh a load, and at 26.11p per kWh that is about 39p each time.

Be aware that Great Britain and Northern Ireland currently use different label scales for dryers. Northern Ireland follows the EU's rescaled A to G label introduced in July 2025, under which early heat pump models land around B or C because the scale was reset to leave headroom. Great Britain has so far kept the older A+++ to D scale, where good heat pump machines show A++ or A+++, though the government has consulted on aligning the two. Check the current rules and label format on the official GOV.UK product standards pages before you compare models, since the regime is mid-transition. Our guide to energy labels explains how to read both formats and convert label figures into pounds.

Two things the label hides: cycle time, which is meaningfully longer on heat pump machines, and the condition assumptions. Label figures assume a properly spun load from a washing machine running at 1,400 rpm or so. If your washer only spins at 1,000 rpm, every dryer type will use noticeably more energy because it starts with wetter clothes. A faster final spin is one of the cheapest drying upgrades there is.

sections.h2: The practical trade-offs to know before buying
sections.html:
Heat pump dryers are not a free lunch. The differences are manageable, but go in with your eyes open.

  • Longer cycles. A full cotton load that takes about 2 hours in a condenser machine often takes 2.5 to 3 hours in a heat pump dryer. If you routinely need to run several loads back to back in an evening, that matters.
  • Cooler drying. Clothes come out warm rather than hot. That is better for fabrics but feels different, and very thick items like towels or duvets occasionally want a second short run.
  • More filter care. As well as the lint filter you empty every load, most heat pump machines have a second foam or mesh filter protecting the evaporator coil. Clean it as the manual says, typically every 5 to 10 loads, or efficiency drops and cycle times stretch. Some dearer models clean the condenser automatically.
  • Cold rooms hurt performance. Most manufacturers specify an operating range of roughly 5 to 30 degrees. In an unheated garage or outbuilding in winter, a heat pump dryer slows down badly and may refuse to perform; a vented machine tolerates the cold better.
  • No vent needed, water tank instead. Like a condenser machine, a heat pump dryer collects water in a tank you empty, or you can plumb the drain in. It can live anywhere with a socket, and it releases far less heat and moisture into the room than older designs.

If the longer cycles or the purchase price put you off entirely, remember the dryer is optional. A heated airer or a dehumidifier in a closed room can dry a load for a similar or lower cost per kilogram, just more slowly; our guide to the cheapest way to dry clothes compares every method side by side.

A heat pump tumble dryer is worth it for any household that uses a dryer regularly. At current prices each full load costs about 44p instead of £1.25, the purchase premium has shrunk to £150 to £250, and at three loads a week the better machine pays for the difference in well under two years, then keeps saving £120 or more every year after that. Over a typical ten-year life that is comfortably £1,000 in avoided electricity for a family-sized laundry habit.

The cases against are narrow but real. If you dry one load a week or less, payback stretches towards five years and a cheap condenser model is defensible. If the machine must live in a cold garage, a vented dryer copes better. And if your current dryer still works, run it into the ground first; the savings justify a premium at replacement time, not an early scrappage. When you do buy, ignore the letter on the label and compare the kWh per 100 cycles figure directly, because that single number, multiplied by your unit rate, is the whole running-cost story.

faq.q: How much does a heat pump tumble dryer cost per load?
faq.a: A typical heat pump dryer uses 1.5 to 2 kWh for a full 8 kg cotton load. At the July to September 2026 price cap rate of 26.11p per kWh, that is roughly 39p to 52p per load, against £1.10 to £1.30 for a vented or condenser dryer.

faq.q: How long does a heat pump dryer take compared with a condenser dryer?
faq.a: Expect 30 to 60 minutes longer per cycle. A full cotton load that takes around 2 hours in a condenser machine commonly takes 2.5 to 3 hours in a heat pump dryer, because moving heat in a closed loop is slower than blasting the drum with a 2.5 kW element.

faq.q: Do heat pump dryers work in a cold garage?
faq.a: Poorly. Most manufacturers specify an operating range of roughly 5 to 30 degrees, and below that the refrigeration circuit struggles, cycles stretch and some machines stop working properly. For an unheated garage or outbuilding, a vented dryer is usually the more reliable choice.

faq.q: Is a heat pump dryer gentler on clothes?
faq.a: Yes. It dries at around 45 to 55 degrees rather than the 70 to 75 degrees of a conventional dryer, which is kinder to elastic, prints and delicate fabrics. The trade-off is that clothes come out warm rather than hot and very thick items occasionally need a little longer.

faq.q: How many loads a week make a heat pump dryer worth the extra cost?
faq.a: Around two loads a week is the tipping point. At that usage the saving versus a condenser dryer is roughly £84 a year at current cap rates, paying back a £200 purchase premium in about two and a half years. At three or more loads a week, payback drops under two years and the decision is straightforward.
